text: We herein present the case of the robotic repair of perineal hernia following perineal one-on-the-essence following X-relevator APR with hysterectomy after neoadjuvant chemioradation for local advanced rectal cancer. A sixty-six-year-old woman, BMI twenty kilogram per square meter, presented with perineal one-on-the-essence and hernia after X-relevator abdominal perineal resection with hysterectomy.

Three 8mm robotic trocars are positioned vertically in the right abdominal quadrants, with an additional 8mm trocar placed in the left flank. A 12mm and a 5mm system port are also positioned in the right quadrants, following the trocar layout used for X-relevator APR.
The robotic surgical system is docked from the patient's left side, with the patient positioned in lithotomy with a slight right tilt.
The surgeon repairs the pelvic floor using biologic mesh, addressing the herniation of small bowel loops through the pelvic outlet.

The surgeon encounters dense adhesions in the pelvic region. A dynamic retractor and table assist are utilized to achieve adequate exposure of the operative field, allowing for better visualization and access during the procedure.

The surgeon uses monopolar scissors and bipolar forceps to perform blunt and sharp dissection, carefully mobilizing small bowel loops while avoiding serosal tears. Minimal energy use is employed during the procedure.
Grass and microscopic contamination of the operative field could there potentially preclude the use of non-absorbable mesh.
The final view of the operative field shows the pelvic region after the adhesion stackdown, with clear visibility of the repaired pelvic floor and surrounding anatomical structures.

The surgeon creates a peritoneal bladder flap after accessing the retropubic space, securing it posteriorly to the sacrum with interrupted absorbable stitches. Dissection continues over the bladder within the rectus space, exposing Cooper's ligaments bilaterally.
The surgeon mobilizes the bladder peritoneal flap laterally, ensuring sufficient tissue is available for posterior fixation.

The surgeon measures the defect intracorporeally and positions a 50x20 cm coated non-absorbable mesh at the pelvic inlet. The mesh is fixed with non-absorbable stitches to the Cooper's ligament bilaterally on the anterior aspect for secure fixation.

The mesh is secured posteriorly at the sacral promontory using absorbable stitches and cyanocrylate glue. The surgeon ensures adequate overlap of the mesh on its lateral aspects to prevent recurrence.
The patient is positioned prone for skin closure, where the surgeon uses interrupted non-absorbable stitches to close the incision. A surgical drain is visibly left in place.
Operative time was two hundred minutes and the patient was discharged on postoperative day five with no complications.
